_metadata.description: Canonical form-factor vocabulary for product physical state. 18 canonical IDs covering DSLD physicalState.langualCodeDescription values and common label variants.
_metadata.purpose: Single source of truth for form-factor normalization, consumed by enricher, v4 router/modules, score_supplements, build_final_db, and the Flutter app.
_metadata.notes: Canonical form-factor vocabulary for product physical state. Owned by the enricher (single normalization stage); v4 router/modules, score_supplements, build_final_db, and Flutter all consume the canonical ID. Aliases cover DSLD `physicalState.langualCodeDescription`, common label-text variants, and v3 enricher's collapsed values. Adding a new physical state requires (1) a new entry here, (2) loader/normalizer test coverage, and (3) consumer review for any scoring impact (e.g. gummy formulation penalty).

form_factors.description: Hard two-piece capsule, gelatin or vegetarian.
Soft-gel liquid-filled capsule. Distinct from hard capsule because softgels are typical for oil-based actives (vitamin D3, omega-3, vitamin E).
Chewable tablet — orally chewed before swallowing. Carries the chewability formulation tradeoffs (sweeteners, palatability fillers) without the gummy gelatin matrix.
Gummy or jelly format. Carries the gummy-formulation penalty in v4 multi/prenatal Formulation scoring due to typical sugar/gelatin matrix and lower active density.
Loose powder, typically reconstituted in water or another liquid.
Oral liquid, syrup, or suspension (not a drops/tincture/spray subform).
Alcohol or glycerin extract dispensed by dropper. Sub-form of liquid but distinct because herbal tinctures dose differently than aqueous liquids.
Lozenge, troche, or pastille — slowly dissolved in the mouth.
Sublingual tablet or strip — dissolves under the tongue. Distinct from lozenge (lozenge dissolves in mouth, sublingual under tongue for direct bloodstream uptake).
Oral drops dispensed by dropper or pipette. Aqueous, not alcohol-based like tincture.
Cream, lotion, gel, salve, or balm applied to the skin. Not orally administered.
Tea bag or infusion bag — steeped in hot water.

Other physical state that does not match any specific category. The DSLD 'Other (e.g. tea bag)' bucket maps to `tea_bag` when the label confirms; this `other` bucket is the residual for unusual forms (suppository, enema, etc.).
Physical state not disclosed or not parseable. Distinct from `other` because `unknown` means data is missing, not that the form is unusual.
